verses.text: When the people complained, it displeased the LORD; the fire of the LORD burned in the uttermost parts of the camp. They cried to Moses; he prayed; the fire was quenched. The place was called Taberah — burning — because the fire burned there. Guidance: The very first complaint of the new journey kindles fire. God's holiness is no joke now — it's traveling with them. Moses prays; the fire goes out. Intercession is already the camp's lifeline.

The mixt multitude fell a-lusting; Israel wept: who shall give us flesh? We remember Egypt — fish, cucumbers, melons, leeks, onions, garlic. Now soul dried away; nothing but manna. Manna was as coriander seed, bdellium colored; ground, baked, taste as fresh oil. Guidance: They remember Egypt as a salad bar. Lust whitewashes slavery — anything but today's gift. Manna fell every morning; they call it nothing. Discontent rewrites the past.

Moses heard them weep at every tent door. Moses to the LORD: why hast Thou afflicted Thy servant? Why laid the burden on me? Have I conceived them? Am I to carry them in my bosom to the land Thou swarest? Not able alone. If Thou deal thus — kill me, that I see not my wretchedness. Guidance: Moses asks God to kill him rather than carry the crowd one more day. The greatest meekness in Scripture cracks under weight. God will answer with help — not rebuke.

The LORD: gather seventy elders, officers; bring to the tabernacle, stand with thee. I will come and talk; take of the spirit on thee, put on them; they shall bear the burden with thee — thou bear not alone. Guidance: God's answer to burnout is not solo strength; it's shared spirit. The same spirit on Moses is distributed to seventy men. Leadership is meant to be carried by a chorus, not a soloist.

The LORD: gather seventy elders, officers; bring to the tabernacle, stand with thee. The same spirit on Moses is distributed to seventy men. Leadership is meant to be carried by a chorus, not a soloist.

Sanctify yourselves for tomorrow — ye shall eat flesh. The LORD will give you flesh — a whole month, till it come out of your nostrils, for ye despised Him. Moses: six hundred thousand footmen — flesh enough? LORD: is My hand waxed short? See whether My word come to pass. Guidance: God gives them what they asked for — a whole month of it. Sometimes judgment comes wearing the shape of the request. Moses doubts the math; God answers with a single question: is My hand short?

Moses told the people. Gathered seventy elders, set around the tabernacle. The LORD came down in the cloud, spoke; took of the spirit on Moses, gave to the seventy. When the spirit rested on them, they prophesied — and did not cease. Guidance: One man's burden becomes seventy men's gift. The spirit isn't divided like a pie — it's shared like flame. Each elder receives the full anointing without diminishing Moses.

When the spirit rested on them, they prophesied — and did not cease. Guidance: One man's burden becomes seventy men's gift. The spirit isn't divided like a pie — it's shared like flame. Each elder receives the full anointing without diminishing Moses.

Eldad and Medad remained in the camp, names written but not gone to the tabernacle. The spirit rested on them; they prophesied. Joshua: forbid them! Moses: enviest thou for my sake? Would God all the LORD's people were prophets, that the LORD would put His spirit on them. Guidance: Joshua wants control; Moses wants spread. The greatest leader yearns for everyone to share his anointing. Pentecost is foreshadowed here: God promises to pour His spirit on all flesh someday.

A wind from the LORD brought quails from the sea, fell two cubits deep. The people gathered all day, night, and next day. While the flesh was between their teeth — the LORD's wrath kindled, He smote them with a great plague. They called the place Kibroth-hattaavah — graves of lust. Then to Hazeroth. Guidance: Be careful what you weep for. They got the quails and lost their lives. Graves of lust — God lets us have what we beg for when we're begging against Him.
